title: Fail-Safe / Fail-Secure Guide
summary: Understand lock behavior during power loss and how that affects life safety, security, and operations.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool helps compare fail-safe and fail-secure behavior for a controlled opening based on the door type, security need, egress requirements, and power-loss expectations.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when selecting electric strikes, maglocks, electrified levers, gate locks, or any opening where power-loss behavior matters.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical access-control terms.

items.label: Opening Type
items.description: The type of door, gate, or controlled opening.
items.whyItMatters: Different openings have different safety and security expectations.
items.commonMistake: Using the same lock behavior everywhere.

items.label: Fail-Safe
items.description: A lock behavior where loss of power unlocks the opening.
items.whyItMatters: Often supports emergency release needs but may reduce security during outages.
items.commonMistake: Using fail-safe where the area must remain secured during power loss.

items.label: Fail-Secure
items.description: A lock behavior where loss of power keeps the opening locked from the controlled side.
items.whyItMatters: Often preserves security but must still satisfy egress requirements.
items.commonMistake: Using fail-secure without confirming legal egress.

items.label: Egress Requirement
items.description: The requirement for people to exit safely.
items.whyItMatters: Life safety requirements override convenience and many security preferences.
items.commonMistake: Assuming access control rules can override egress requirements.

items.label: Power Backup
items.description: Battery or UPS support for the locking circuit.
items.whyItMatters: Backup power changes how long normal lock behavior can continue.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring outage duration and battery maintenance.

items.label: Security Priority
items.description: How important it is for the opening to remain secure during abnormal conditions.
items.whyItMatters: High-security areas may require different hardware or backup strategy.
items.commonMistake: Choosing hardware based only on normal daily operation.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the behavior aligns with the likely operational need. Watch means confirm life-safety and site requirements. Risk means the lock behavior may conflict with egress, security, or emergency expectations.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: simplify the design, add capacity, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most access-control design problems come from underestimating how hardware, users, schedules, exceptions, and maintenance effort interact over time.
bullets: Designing only for the initial install instead of the system lifecycle.
Ignoring exceptions, expansion, or administrative workload.
Assuming manufacturer capacity limits are the same as practical design limits.
Failing to document assumptions before the system is built.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, code review, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits still matter.
Local code and life-safety requirements still apply.
Site conditions may change the final design.
A simpler design is usually easier to support, audit, and explain.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, code compliance review, manufacturer validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual product and platform being installed.
Confirm owner requirements and operating procedures.
Document assumptions before implementation.
